content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Claudia Quintana 
Vallejo City Attorney 
555 Santa Clara Street 
Vallejo, CA 94590 
RE: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-12-0lJa 
Dear Ms. Quintana: 
November 14, 2013 
This letter responds to your request for clarification of advice provided to your office on 
behalf of Vallejo Council member Stephanie Gomes (Nagel Advice Letter, No. A-12-013) 
regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")' 
Please note, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 
18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct 
that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to 
prospective actions. Moreover, the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") 
does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (/11 re Oglesby ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) 
Finally, this letter is based solely on the provisions of the Act. The Commission has no 
jurisdiction to advise on Section I 090 until Assembly Bill 1090 becomes effective on 
January I, 2014. 
You have asked us if the following additional facts change the conclusion in Nagel or 
cause us to reconsider the Nagel advice. We consider each set of new facts individually. 
l. Some retired police captains, while employed with the City, were represented by CAMP; 
some retired and some current police captains were and are represented by VPOA; and some

"blended" CalPERS Account which is funded by current Employee and City contributions 
and for which CalPERS sets the required City Contribution level on an annual basis 
completely independent from any City action.

the amount of conce!->sions obtained from one labor group may affect the amount of

bargaining group arc conducted separately from each other, but all negotiations with all 
of the groups arc currently ongoing.

two others retired as police captains while being represented by CAMP. Four retired as police captains while the job 
classification was being represented by VPOA. and two police captains arc active employees represented by VPOA. 
Two police captains retired al a time when the job classification was regarded as exempt and not within any 
bargaining unit.

sections.question: Does the addition of facts regarding retired police captains represented by different unions and their pension benefits affect the conclusions in the previous Nagel Advice Letter (A-12-013) concerning conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion: Adding these facts to those in Nagel does not affect the conclusions in that formal written advice. The purpose behind the exception in Regulation 18705.5(b) is to prohibit public officials from participating in decisions that would uniquely affect the governmental income of the official or the official's spouse, but to allow the official to participate in decisions that affected a class that included the official's spouse. The retired police captains represented by CAMP establish a class of which the official's spouse is a member, and therefore the spouse would not be uniquely affected. Accordingly, the Commission confirms the formal advice in the Nagel advice letter.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether additional facts concerning representation of retired police captains by different unions and their CalPERS pension benefits affect the conflict-of-interest analysis under the Political Reform Act, specifically regarding whether a City Council member's spouse in the police captain classification is uniquely affected by municipal decisions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The additional facts regarding different union representations and pension benefits do not change the previous Nagel advice conclusion because retired police captains represented by CAMP form a class of which the Council member's spouse is a member, meaning the spouse is not uniquely affected by municipal decisions. The spouse's pension income would be affected as part of a larger affected class rather than as an individual, consistent with the Regulation 18705.5(b) exception.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a follow-up to previous Nagel advice letter (A-12-013). Clear QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections embedded in prose format. Document includes discussion of specific regulatory provisions and legal analysis. Limitation noted that Commission advises only on prospective conduct, not past conduct.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the addition of facts regarding retired police captains represented by different unions and their pension benefits affect the conclusions in the previous Nagel Advice Letter (A-12-013) concerning conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: Adding these facts to those in Nagel does not affect the conclusions in that formal written advice. The purpose behind the exception in Regulation 18705.5(b) is to prohibit public officials from participating in decisions that would uniquely affect the governmental income of the official or the official's spouse, but to allow the official to participate in decisions that affected a class that included the official's spouse. The retired police captains represented by CAMP establish a class of which the official's spouse is a member, and therefore the spouse would not be uniquely affected. Accordingly, the Commission confirms the formal advice in the Nagel advice letter.

embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter clarifies and confirms a prior advice letter (Nagel A-12-013) regarding conflict-of-interest provisions under the Political Reform Act concerning a Vallejo Council member's spouse who is a retired police captain, specifically analyzing whether additional facts about union representation and CalPERS pension benefits change the legal conclusions.
